1 Information Literacy and Information Ethics course

First step involves the conversion of logs into the distint 14 features. 

The authors create 3 distinct datasets, one for each moment of prediction:

W4, W8 and W13.

Dataset imbalance was addressed by mixing undersampling and oversampling. This process was repeated 30 times on different seeds.

Temporal features are also derived.

Authors mention 330 portfolios but only 300 participants.

Authors make comparison of performance between models trained including with time-dependent variables and other not including them.

Time-dependent variables reveal themselves as very helpful and Course_LoginTime ranking as the most important feature throughout.

On a first read, the Tree-based algorithms achieve better performance than LR. 

On a second, it is evident that time-dependent variables are very important and improve performance. 

Finally, Ensemble models (AdaBoost) outperform single base learners.

Iearly prediction is possible at the 4th week and that predictive performance does not seem to improve that much with the addition of time.

Small dataset featuring data from a single online course.

No use of additional sources of data. 

Unclear use of the ensemble → reading hints at stacking between AdaBoost and DT algorithm. However, the DT being the weak learner seems more feasible.

Measurement of accuracy as the metric to look for seems unwise given imbalanced nature. 

Possibly overfitting.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: Authors start by mentioning that other LA works thus far do indeed use logs to predict success but neglect the practical aspects of an early warning system: predicting early in order to support student success.

text.content: Authors start by mentioning that other LA works thus far do indeed use logs to predict success but neglect the practical aspects of an early warning system: predicting early in order to support student success.

rich_text.plain_text: Authors start by mentioning that other LA works thus far do indeed use logs to predict success but neglect the practical aspects of an early warning system: predicting early in order to support student success.

blocks.text: Thus, the authors extract log data and use it to create an early warning system. Time-dependent variables have shown to be especially useful in this endeavor. Moreover, a CART supplemented by an AdaBoost showed to be the best model out of the ones attempted.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: LMS are, nowadays, a useful tool to identify learners who may be at risk. Prior work has shown that using activity logs AND learner data that is stored on the LMS may be enough to create effective systems for this.

text.content: LMS are, nowadays, a useful tool to identify learners who may be at risk. Prior work has shown that using activity logs AND learner data that is stored on the LMS may be enough to create effective systems for this.

rich_text.plain_text: LMS are, nowadays, a useful tool to identify learners who may be at risk. Prior work has shown that using activity logs AND learner data that is stored on the LMS may be enough to create effective systems for this.

blocks.text: A bigger challenge, however, is creating a model that integrates the wealth of online data that is now generated and, more importantly, actually having an LMS with built-in capacity to leverage the data and make predictions. This study attempts to make prediction using learning portfolio datasets while a fully online course is still in progress. In other words, early prediction that considers time-dependent variables and other activities undertaken by the students.

blocks.text: The Research Questions are as follows:

blocks.text: To answer the questions, authors use data from 330 learning portfolios of students attending a fully online course. The data was collected as the course was taking place. This data was then used to compare different candidate ML algorithms for identification of learner’s at risk.

blocks.text: Literature Review

text.content: Literature Review

rich_text.plain_text: Literature Review

blocks.text: For this paper, learning portfolios are understood as collections of events and activities undertaken by the student. Now that it is possible to make online creation of portfolios by looking at the history of learning activities, these portfolios are more and more popular tools in the prediction of student performance. However, there are still few studies looking at the effectiveness of early warning systems created in this way.

blocks.text: Very few studies actually use time-dependent representations. This means that most of the models use whole-semester information that hinders the ability of a model to be used as an early warning system → little to no time to help student.

blocks.text: Data and Methods

text.content: Data and Methods

rich_text.plain_text: Data and Methods

blocks.text:
Course has 2 editions and takes the whole semester. Students are required to attend online classes and watch specific videos during specific intervals. The LMS stores the interactions as logs, meaning that, to be usable, the data must undergo some transformations that resulted into 14 distinct features.

Users are described by 4 types of feature: Logins, Online material usage, assignments status and discussion forum status. The authors claim to have 330 portfolios but they only recruited 300 students. Moreover, problem is extremely imbalanced (284: pass vs 16 fail). In order to include temporal components into the models, the authors create 3 distinct datasets → one for each moment of prediction (W4, W8 or W13).

text.content:

blocks.text: These datasets are then used to train and compare different classifiers on WeKa → 3 baseline models in C4.5, CART and Logistic Regression and one ensemble model in AdaBoost. Dataset imbalance was addressed by mixing undersampling and oversampling. This process was repeated 30 times on different seeds. So, in essence, the authors do 30 repetitions of 10-Fold Cross-Validation. Average performances are stored and compared using:

blocks.text: Results

text.content: Results

rich_text.plain_text: Results

blocks.text: As far as results go, they can briefly be summarized as follows:

text.content: As far as results go, they can briefly be summarized as follows:

rich_text.plain_text: As far as results go, they can briefly be summarized as follows:

blocks.text:
Comparison between baseline algorithms on the 3 datasets

In the first experiment, the authors make a comparison between C4.5, CART and LR on their average scores across the 3 datasets → W4, W8 and W13. Key observations show that Tree-based models perform better (CART on W4 and W8 and C4.5 on W13) → differences do not seem to be statistically significant though.

text.content:
Comparison between baseline algorithms on the 3 datasets

In the first experiment, the authors make a comparison between C4.5, CART and LR on their average scores across the 3 datasets → W4, W8 and W13. Key observations show that Tree-based models perform better (CART on W4 and W8 and C4.5 on W13) → differences do not seem to be statistically significant though.

rich_text.plain_text:
Comparison between baseline algorithms on the 3 datasets

In the first experiment, the authors make a comparison between C4.5, CART and LR on their average scores across the 3 datasets → W4, W8 and W13. Key observations show that Tree-based models perform better (CART on W4 and W8 and C4.5 on W13) → differences do not seem to be statistically significant though.

blocks.text:
Addition of Time-Dependent Variables

On the tree-based models the authors remove time-dependent variables from the datasets and make a comparison of performances using those features vs not using them.  Unsurprisingly, the time-dependent features reveal themselves as important an, for that reason, models that include them have better performance.

text.content:
Addition of Time-Dependent Variables

On the tree-based models the authors remove time-dependent variables from the datasets and make a comparison of performances using those features vs not using them.  Unsurprisingly, the time-dependent features reveal themselves as important an, for that reason, models that include them have better performance.

rich_text.plain_text:
Addition of Time-Dependent Variables

On the tree-based models the authors remove time-dependent variables from the datasets and make a comparison of performances using those features vs not using them.  Unsurprisingly, the time-dependent features reveal themselves as important an, for that reason, models that include them have better performance.

blocks.text: Finally, the authors make 2 Ensemble models that, from my guess are AdaBoost implementations, the first an AdaBoost with C4.5 as weak learner and the second having CART as the weak learner. The best overall performances are achieved with the ensemble classifiers.

text.content: Finally, the authors make 2 Ensemble models that, from my guess are AdaBoost implementations, the first an AdaBoost with C4.5 as weak learner and the second having CART as the weak learner. The best overall performances are achieved with the ensemble classifiers.

blocks.text: In general, results show that early prediction is possible at the 4th week and that predictive performance does not seem to improve that much with the passing of the weeks.

blocks.text:
Final sections of the paper discusses the how do I warn the student part of the problem with EWS - something that may be useful later on, but is not for now. Short-version, the school establishes an early warning agent that warns both professor and student.

There is also a flowchart for learning improvement. You can see it here for the lulz.
